1.pericope_label: To the Elders and the Flock
1.synthesis: Peter exhorts the elders as a fellow elder (sympresbyteros), a witness of the sufferings of Christ, and a partaker in the glory to be revealed: 'shepherd the flock of God that is among you, exercising oversight, not under compulsion but willingly, as God would have you; not for shameful gain but eagerly; not domineering over those in your charge, but being examples to the flock' (vv.1–3). The motive and reward: 'And when the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory' (v.4). The reciprocal call: 'Likewise, you who are younger, be subject to the elders. Clothe yourselves, all of you, with humility toward one another, for "God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble"' (v.5).
1.historical_context: Peter's self-identification as sympresbyteros — fellow elder — rather than apostle is a deliberate rhetorical move: he places himself alongside those he addresses as a peer in pastoral labor, not above them as a commanding superior. The three contrasts — compulsion/willing, shameful gain/eagerly, domineering/examples — map onto the three perennial corruptions of pastoral ministry: coercion, greed, and hierarchical abuse. The archipoimenos (Chief Shepherd) of v.4 is a hapax legomenon in the NT — appearing nowhere else — and places Christ above all under-shepherds as the ultimate authority by whom their ministry will be evaluated. The 'unfading crown of glory' contrasts with the perishable laurel wreath of athletic victory. The Proverbs 3:34 (LXX) citation in v.5 grounds the mutual humility call in a divine principle: God's active opposition to the proud is as certain as his grace toward the humble.
1.literary_structure: The section is structured as the pastoral counterpart to the household code of chs. 2–3: just as Peter addressed wives, husbands, servants, and citizens, he now addresses leaders and followers within the community. The elder instruction (vv.1–4) is organized around three anti-types (what not to be) and their corresponding virtues, all culminating in the Chief Shepherd's appearing as the accountability horizon. The younger-subject instruction (v.5a) is brief but anchors submission in the elder-structure Peter has just described. The mutual humility call (v.5b) then universalizes the principle: humility toward one another is not merely a leader-follower dynamic but the community's posture before God, grounded in Proverbs.
1.theological_application: Gill on the three contrasts of vv.2–3: 'Not by constraint, or as if forced to it, through fear of censure, or for shame … not for filthy lucre's sake, not out of covetousness … and not as lords over God's heritage: they are not to lord it over the consciences of men, or usurp a dominion over their faith.' The three corruptions Gill identifies are internal (coercive motivation), mercenary (financial motivation), and domineering (power motivation) — and the remedy for each is given in the same verse: willing service, eager service, exemplary service. Matthew Henry on the Chief Shepherd's appearing: 'The prospect of Christ's appearing to be their judge and rewarder should make ministers faithful and diligent in their work; they will receive not a fading laurel but an unfading crown of glory from him.' The accountability horizon of the Chief Shepherd's return transforms pastoral labor from social performance into eschatological stewardship.
1.christology: The archipoimenos — Chief Shepherd — who will appear in v.4 is the same 'Shepherd and Overseer of your souls' of 2:25, creating a structural inclusion around the letter's central pastoral image: Christ is both the one who gathers the straying sheep (2:25) and the one who will evaluate those charged with tending the gathered flock (5:4). The under-shepherds' ministry is derivative and accountable: they shepherd God's flock, not their own, on behalf of the Chief Shepherd who purchased that flock with his own blood. Peter as 'witness of the sufferings of Christ' (v.1) grounds his authority to address elders not in apostolic rank but in personal witness of the suffering that makes the Chief Shepherd's pastoral care credible — he who suffered for the sheep is the one whose shepherds must not seek to avoid suffering for their charge.

6.pericope_label: Be Sober-Minded and Resist the Devil
6.synthesis: 'Humble yourselves, therefore, under the mighty hand of God, so that at the proper time he may exalt you, casting all your anxieties on him, because he cares for you' (vv.6–7). The warning: 'Be sober-minded; be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour. Resist him, firm in your faith, knowing that the same kinds of suffering are being experienced by your brotherhood throughout the world' (vv.8–9). The promise: 'And after you have suffered a little while, the God of all grace, who has called you to his eternal glory in Christ, will himself restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish you. To him be the dominion forever and ever. Amen' (vv.10–11).
6.historical_context: The 'mighty hand of God' is an Exodus image: in Exodus 13:3 and repeatedly, God's 'mighty hand' is what delivered Israel from Egypt, the symbol of divine sovereign power working on behalf of his people. Humbling oneself under that hand is not a posture of defeat but of trust in a power that has already proved its reliability. The casting of anxieties echoes Psalm 55:22: 'Cast your burden on the LORD, and he will sustain you.' The devil as antidikos (legal opponent/adversary) in v.8 evokes the courtroom: the devil is not merely a personal tempter but a prosecutorial accuser who seeks to devour — legal language for destroying one's case. The four verbs of v.10 (restore, confirm, strengthen, establish) are a cluster of consolidation terms describing what God's grace accomplishes after the period of suffering.
6.literary_structure: The section moves in three movements: the posture of humility under God's hand with the release of anxiety (vv.6–7); the vigilant resistance of the adversary grounded in shared brotherhood suffering (vv.8–9); and the restoration promise grounded in God's calling and grace (vv.10–11). The humility of v.6 is the answer to the pride that the devil can exploit: the one who has cast anxieties on God and submitted to his sovereign hand has taken away the devil's primary lever. The 'brotherhood throughout the world' (v.9) is significant — the suffering community in Pontus and Bithynia is not uniquely disadvantaged but shares the experience of the universal church, which normalizes their suffering rather than suggesting divine disfavor.
6.theological_application: Gill on casting anxieties on God: 'Not that they should be without all care about temporal things, or spiritual ones; but that they should not be anxiously careful, not distressingly careful; they should not be burdened with care … they should cast all their cares on God, and leave them with him.' The cast is not passive resignation but active trust: handing the weight of care to one whose care for the community is declared in the same breath. Gill on the devil as roaring lion: 'He is compared to a roaring lion, which is his temper and disposition; he is fierce, wrathful, and cruel, and has a great deal of strength and power … and in respect of his voraciousness and cruelty to the saints, seeking to devour their faith, their graces, their peace and comfort.' Matthew Henry on the four restoration verbs (v.10): 'God himself will do it — not leave it to any subordinate agent; he will restore, make you more perfect, supply what is lacking; he will stablish, settle you on a firm foundation; he will strengthen, make you more vigorous and active; he will settle you, fix you firmly so you no longer waver.' The four verbs describe progressive consolidation rather than a single act.
6.christology: 'The God of all grace, who has called you to his eternal glory in Christ' (v.10) — the calling to glory is christological in both ground and mode. God's grace is 'all grace' because the one through whom it flows is the one in whom all fullness dwells. The 'eternal glory in Christ' is the glory that Christ entered through suffering and resurrection, the glory that believers will share when the Chief Shepherd appears (v.4). The 'after you have suffered a little while' (v.10) echoes the letter's repeated pattern: suffering precedes glory, as it did for Christ. The four restoration verbs describe what God does after suffering, just as he did for Christ after the cross. The doxology of v.11 ('to him be the dominion forever and ever') attributes to God through Christ the sovereign power that makes the devil's roaring ultimately futile: the adversary prowls, but dominion belongs to God.

12.synthesis: 'By Silvanus, a faithful brother as I regard him, I have written briefly to you, exhorting and declaring that this is the true grace of God. Stand firm in it' (v.12). Greetings from the community at Babylon (Rome), and from Mark: 'She who is at Babylon, who is likewise chosen, sends you greetings, and so does Mark, my son' (v.13). The closing: 'Greet one another with the kiss of love. Peace to all of you who are in Christ' (v.14).
12.historical_context: Silvanus (Silas) served as Peter's amanuensis — the letter-writer who took dictation or was given freedom to express the apostle's thought in his own Greek style. His identification as 'faithful' suggests personal commendation to the recipients. 'Babylon' is the standard early Christian code-name for Rome, used also in Revelation 17:5: Rome as the great empire-city, the new Babylon of captivity. John Mark — 'my son' — is the same Mark associated with Peter in Acts and with the Gospel of Mark, which early tradition consistently connected to Peter's preaching. The 'kiss of love' was a liturgical practice in early Christian worship, mentioned also by Paul (Romans 16:16; 1 Corinthians 16:20).
12.literary_structure: The three-verse close performs the letter's theology in miniature. Silvanus authenticates the letter and summarizes its purpose: exhortation and declaration of the true grace of God — the two movements of the entire letter. The greeting from the Roman community and from Mark shows that the scattered exiles in Asia Minor are part of a wider network of the chosen. The kiss of love enacts the brotherly love commanded in 1:22 and 3:8. The benediction 'peace to all of you who are in Christ' locates peace in a person and in a community defined by their location in him — closing the letter where it began, with the grace and peace that belong to the scattered elect.
12.theological_application: Gill on 'the true grace of God' as the content of the letter: 'This is indeed the true grace of God, in distinction from that which the false teachers boasted of, who only carried a form of godliness without the power; for the Gospel, which is a declaration of the grace of God, is here meant; and which Peter preached and wrote to them, as the genuine and true Gospel of the grace of God.' The 'stand firm in it' is therefore not merely an emotional encouragement but an epistemic and theological summons: the grace declared in this letter is the genuine article, not a substitute, and standing firm in it means refusing to be moved by the false alternatives that the devil, the hostile world, or errant teachers might offer. Matthew Henry on the closing peace-wish: 'Peace, in Scripture, includes all good things — safety, prosperity, happiness, and especially reconciliation with God through Christ, which is the foundation of all other peace.'
12.christology: 'Peace to all of you who are in Christ' (v.14) — the closing benediction locates peace in a person, not merely a state. The phrase 'in Christ,' rare in 1 Peter but used here at the letter's close, draws together the entire argument: those who are 'in Christ' are the scattered elect who have been born again to a living hope through his resurrection, who follow his suffering as their pattern, who will share his glory at his appearing, and who now receive his peace as their benediction. The letter opens with grace and peace from God the Father through the sanctifying Spirit (1:2) and closes with peace to those who are in Christ — the triune movement of the letter's theology ends with the community gathered into the Son. He who bore their sins, modeled their suffering, and will appear as their Chief Shepherd is the one in whom they now stand and in whom they find the peace that the world cannot give.
